constitution_day.intro: Poland's Constitution Day on May 3rd commemorates the Constitution of May 3rd, 1791 — Europe's first modern constitution and the world's second after the United States.
constitution_day.history: Poland adopted Europe's first modern written constitution on May 3rd, 1791 — well before France. It was meant to reform the weakening noble republic, abolished the liberum veto and strengthened the burgher class. But only 14 months later Russian troops invaded, and Poland lost statehood in three partitions (1772, 1793, 1795). Only after Poland's restoration in 1918 did the day become a national holiday. During communism it was banned; since 1990 it has been an official holiday again.
constitution_day.traditions: In Warsaw a major military parade is held in front of the palace, and patriots wear the Polish flag or red-and-white ribbons. Schools organize ceremonies with songs and poems. Together with May 1st (Labour Day) and May 2nd (Flag Day), it forms the popular ‘Majówka' weekend — Poland's informal May break.
funFacts: Poland's constitution of 1791 was the world's second-oldest — after the U.S. (1787) and before France (1791, but later in the year).
The ‘Majówka' is one of Poland's most travel-intensive weekends — many city residents flee to the countryside.
During the communist era, May 3rd was replaced by July 22nd (founding day of the People's Republic).
Pope John Paul II — a Pole — called the constitution of 1791 ‘the last work of free Poles before the long sleep'.
constitution_day.imageCredit: Photo: Scotch Mist · CC BY-SA 4.0 via Wikimedia Commons

independence_day.intro: Polish Independence Day on November 11th celebrates the restoration of the Polish state in 1918 — after 123 years without statehood.
independence_day.history: Poland was carved up between Russia, Prussia and Austria in three partitions in 1772, 1793 and 1795 — statehood ended. With the end of World War I, all three occupying powers collapsed. On November 11th, 1918 Józef Piłsudski took power in Warsaw and proclaimed the restored Republic. During the communist era the holiday was banned; only in 1989 was it reintroduced.
independence_day.traditions: The highlight is the great ‘March of Independence' in Warsaw, in which hundreds of thousands of people march with Polish flags. There are official military ceremonies at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier and at Piłsudski's mausoleum. Families wear red-and-white ribbons, children learn patriotic songs. Many cities host folk festivals with traditional foods like pierogi and bigos.
funFacts: Józef Piłsudski — the central founding father — later became a controversial figure as authoritarian head of state.
Poland's Independence Day coincides with Allied Armistice Day (November 11th, 1918) — a historical coincidence.
The Polish flag was officially codified in 1919 — white stands for purity, red for valor.
On November 11th flags fly outside most Polish homes — a more visible public patriotism than on many other days.
independence_day.imageCredit: Photo: Chancellery of the Prime Minister of Poland · CC BY 3.0 pl via Wikimedia Commons
